clean_story: Bright spots. Dark shadows. "MRI contrasts" are the differences in brightness on scans—like a flashlight revealing textures in a dark room. Tissues don’t glow on their own. The machine’s magnet aligns water molecules in your body like compass needles. Radio waves knock them off-kilter—then they "relax" back, emitting signals. Fat and water relax at different speeds. Bone stays dark (few water molecules). Fluid glows bright (water sloshes freely). Doctors tweak settings to highlight what matters:T1-weighted—fat shines (good for spotting tumors in fatty tissue). T2-weighted—water lights up (reveals brain swelling or cysts). Contrast dye (gadolinium) acts like a highlighter—leaking into abnormal areas, making blood vessels or inflammation pop. Three related subjects are magnetic fields, tissue density, and signal processing.
